title: The 10 Core Principles: Complete Guide
description: Master all 10 Core Principles for effective internal auditing with real-world applications

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The 10 Core Principles define what effective internal auditing looks like. The exam frequently tests whether an action supports or violates specific principles. You must know ALL 10 and be able to apply them to scenarios.

sections.title: Overview: The 10 Core Principles
sections.content:
**All 10 must be present and operating effectively for IA to be considered effective.**

1. **Demonstrates integrity** - Honest, brave, prudent
2. **Demonstrates competence and due professional care** - Skilled, diligent
3. **Is objective and free from undue influence** - Unbiased assessments
4. **Aligns with strategies, objectives, and risks** - Relevant focus
5. **Is appropriately positioned and adequately resourced** - Authority & resources
6. **Demonstrates quality and continuous improvement** - QAIP
7. **Communicates effectively** - Clear, timely, relevant
8. **Provides risk-based assurance** - Risk-prioritized coverage
9. **Is insightful, proactive, and future-focused** - Forward-looking
10. **Promotes organizational improvement** - Drives change

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Core Principles
sections.content:
**"I Can Only Aim For Quality Communication — Inspiring Progressive Organizations"**

**I** - Integrity
**C** - Competence
**O** - Objectivity
**A** - Alignment
**F** - (properly) Positioned ("Fit for purpose")
**Q** - Quality
**C** - Communication
**I** - (risk-based) Insurance (assurance)
**P** - Proactive
**O** - Organizational improvement

sections.title: Principles 1-3: The Character Principles
sections.content:
**These define WHO internal auditors must be:**

**1. Integrity** — The foundation of trust
• Honest in all professional matters
• Courageous to report difficult findings
• Actions consistent with values
• Example: Reporting fraud even when politically inconvenient

**2. Competence & Due Care** — The skill requirement
• Possessing necessary knowledge
• Applying professional skepticism
• Continuous learning (CPD)
• Example: Getting cybersecurity training before IT audits

**3. Objectivity** — The independence imperative
• Free from undue influence
• No financial conflicts of interest
• Balanced, unbiased assessments
• Example: Declining to audit a department you previously managed

sections.title: Principles 4-6: The Structural Principles
sections.content:
**These define HOW internal audit must be organized:**

**4. Alignment** — Connected to strategy
• Audit plan reflects organizational risks
• CAE understands business strategy
• Resources directed at highest-risk areas
• Example: Adjusting audit plan when M&A activity increases

**5. Positioning & Resources** — Authority to act
• CAE reports functionally to the board
• Sufficient budget and staffing
• Unrestricted access to information
• Example: Direct access between CAE and Audit Committee chair

**6. Quality** — Continuous improvement
• QAIP with internal and external assessments
• Conformance with Standards
• Benchmarking against best practices
• Example: Implementing recommendations from external QA review

sections.title: Principles 7-10: The Impact Principles
sections.content:
**These define WHAT internal audit must deliver:**

**7. Communication** — Getting the message across
• Timely, clear, and relevant reporting
• Immediate escalation of critical findings
• Appropriate level of detail for audience
• Example: Flash reports for emergent risks

**8. Risk-Based Assurance** — Focused coverage
• Risk assessment drives the audit plan
• Higher-risk areas get more attention
• Dynamic response to changing risks
• Example: Reprioritizing to cover new cyber threats

**9. Insightful & Proactive** — Looking ahead
• Identify emerging risks
• Use data analytics for deeper insights
• Catalyst for change, not just reviewer
• Example: Alerting board to supply-chain risks before disruption

**10. Organizational Improvement** — Driving value
• Practical, actionable recommendations
• Follow-up on implementation
• Contributing to organizational learning
• Example: Helping management redesign broken processes

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Partial Achievement
sections.content:
Exam will test: "If 8 of 10 Core Principles are effective, is internal audit effective?"

**Answer: NO.** ALL 10 must be present and operating effectively. Partial achievement = not fully effective.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• All 10 Core Principles must be effective — no exceptions
• They cover character (1-3), structure (4-6), and impact (7-10)
• Exam tests application — match scenarios to correct principle
• Core Principles inform interpretation of the Standards
• Use them as a self-assessment framework

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following scenarios BEST demonstrates a violation of the 'Objectivity' principle, one of the three character principles?
options: An internal auditor reports a minor control deficiency to management, even though it may reflect poorly on their department's performance.
An internal auditor accepts a small gift (e.g., a pen) from a department they are auditing.
An internal auditor, whose spouse works in the finance department, is assigned to audit that department.
An internal auditor recommends improvements to a process that they previously helped design.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The BEST answer is C. Objectivity is compromised when personal relationships or other factors could unduly influence the auditor's judgment. Having a spouse in the department being audited creates a potential conflict of interest. While accepting gifts (B) is generally discouraged, it is not as direct a violation of objectivity as the spousal relationship. A (Integrity) and D (Independence) relate to other principles. A, while potentially affecting the auditor's career, demonstrates integrity, not a lack of objectivity. D, while potentially affecting independence, is not as direct a violation of objectivity as the spousal relationship.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which scenario creates the GREATEST potential for bias in the auditor's judgment.

reveal.question: Name the three 'Structural Principles' of Internal Auditing.
reveal.answer: The three structural principles are: Alignment, Positioning & Resources, and Governance.

sections.title: Knowledge Check: Impact of Ineffective Principles

knowledgeCheck.question: According to the lesson, what is the consequence if one or more of the 10 Core Principles of Internal Auditing are NOT present and operating effectively?
options: The internal audit activity may still be considered effective, but with some limitations.
The internal audit activity is considered partially effective, requiring remediation.
The internal audit activity cannot be considered effective.
The internal audit activity will be considered effective with an explanation of the limitation.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The lesson states that ALL 10 principles must be present and operating effectively for the internal audit activity to be considered effective. Therefore, the correct answer is C.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the 'Overview' section of the lesson - what is the key takeaway?

title: IPPF Framework: Complete Structure
description: Navigate the International Professional Practices Framework — mandatory vs. recommended guidance

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The IPPF is the authoritative framework for internal audit practice globally. Exam questions test which elements are mandatory vs. recommended and the structure of the Standards. Knowing the framework's architecture is fundamental.

sections.title: IPPF Structure Overview
sections.content:
**The IPPF has two categories of guidance:**

**MANDATORY GUIDANCE (must comply):**
1. Mission of Internal Audit
2. Core Principles for the Professional Practice of Internal Auditing
3. Definition of Internal Auditing
4. Code of Ethics
5. International Standards for the Professional Practice of Internal Auditing

**RECOMMENDED GUIDANCE (should follow):**
1. Implementation Guides
2. Supplemental Guidance (Practice Guides, etc.)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Mandatory Elements
sections.content:
**"My Car Drives Carefully on Streets"**

**M** - Mission
**C** - Core Principles
**D** - Definition
**C** - Code of Ethics
**S** - Standards

All five are MANDATORY. Everything else is recommended.

sections.title: Standards Architecture
sections.content:
**The Standards are organized into two main series:**

**1000 Series: Attribute Standards**
• Describe characteristics of audit organizations and individuals
• 1000: Purpose, Authority, Responsibility (Charter)
• 1100: Independence and Objectivity
• 1200: Proficiency and Due Professional Care
• 1300: Quality Assurance and Improvement Program

**2000 Series: Performance Standards**
• Describe nature of activities and quality criteria
• 2000: Managing the Internal Audit Activity
• 2100: Nature of Work
• 2200: Engagement Planning
• 2300: Performing the Engagement
• 2400: Communicating Results
• 2500: Monitoring Progress
• 2600: Communicating Acceptance of Risks

sections.title: Implementation Standards: A and C
sections.content:
**Implementation Standards expand on Attribute and Performance Standards:**

• **".A"** suffix = Applies to **Assurance** activities
  Example: Standard 2130.A1 - Assurance on controls

• **".C"** suffix = Applies to **Consulting** activities
  Example: Standard 2130.C1 - Consulting on controls

The base standard applies to BOTH assurance and consulting. Implementation Standards provide service-specific requirements.

sections.title: Claiming Conformance
sections.content:
**Key Rules for Conformance:**

• Must comply with ALL mandatory guidance to claim conformance
• Recommended guidance is helpful but NOT required for conformance
• If law prohibits compliance with a Standard → must disclose:
  - The non-conformance
  - The reason (legal prohibition)
  - The impact on the activity
• CAE must report on conformance to senior management and the board

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• IPPF = Mission + Core Principles + Definition + Ethics + Standards (mandatory) + Implementation Guides + Practice Guides (recommended)
• Attribute Standards (1000s) = who we are; Performance Standards (2000s) = what we do
• "A" suffix = Assurance; "C" suffix = Consulting
• Must comply with ALL mandatory elements to claim conformance
• Non-conformance due to law must be disclosed

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following elements of the IPPF represents MANDATORY guidance that internal audit functions MUST comply with?
options: Implementation Guides
Supplemental Guidance
International Standards for the Professional Practice of Internal Auditing
Practice Advisories
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The International Standards for the Professional Practice of Internal Auditing are part of the Mandatory Guidance within the IPPF. The other options fall under Recommended Guidance, which is encouraged but not required.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what happens if an auditor *doesn't* follow a certain type of guidance.

reveal.question: The 1100 series of the International Standards focuses on what key attribute of internal auditors?
reveal.answer: Independence and Objectivity. This series addresses organizational independence and individual objectivity, which are crucial for unbiased assessments.

rows.label: Primary Goal
values: Provide independent assessments of governance, risk management, and control processes.

Provide advisory and related client service activities, the nature and scope of which are agreed with the client, and which are intended to add value and improve an organization’s governance, risk management, and control processes.

rows.label: Objectivity
values: Auditor maintains objectivity by independently evaluating evidence.
Auditor provides advice and guidance; less emphasis on independent assessment.

title: Proficiency: IT Knowledge & Fraud Awareness
description: Master proficiency requirements — IT competencies, fraud detection, and specialization needs

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Standard 1210 covers proficiency requirements that apply to EVERY internal auditor. The exam specifically tests IT knowledge requirements and fraud awareness even for non-specialist auditors. This is a commonly missed area!

sections.title: Collective Proficiency (Standard 1210)
sections.content:
**Key Concept: Collective vs. Individual Proficiency**

The internal audit activity must COLLECTIVELY possess or obtain:
• Knowledge, skills, and other competencies to perform responsibilities

Not every individual needs every skill, but the TEAM overall must have or be able to access the needed expertise.

**The CAE's role:**
• Assess staff competencies against needs
• Identify gaps and plan development
• Obtain external resources when needed
• Ensure right people on right engagements

sections.title: IT Knowledge Requirements (1210.A3)
sections.content:
**ALL internal auditors need sufficient knowledge of:**

• Key information technology risks
• Key IT controls
• Technology-based audit techniques

**This does NOT mean everyone must be an IT expert!**

But they should understand:
• IT general controls (access, change management, operations)
• Data integrity and security basics
• How to use CAATs (Computer-Assisted Audit Techniques)
• Red flags indicating IT control failures
• When to bring in IT audit specialists

sections.title: Fraud Knowledge Requirements (1210.A2)
sections.content:
**ALL internal auditors must have sufficient knowledge to:**

• Evaluate the RISK of fraud
• Identify INDICATORS (red flags) of fraud
• Understand how fraud is managed by the organization

**They are NOT expected to:**
• Be fraud investigation experts
• Conduct complete fraud investigations independently
• Have the same expertise as a CFE (Certified Fraud Examiner)

**What to do when fraud is suspected:**
1. Extend audit procedures to gather indicators
2. Document observations carefully
3. Notify the CAE immediately
4. CAE determines next steps (legal, investigation, board)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Individual vs. Activity
sections.content:
The exam will try to trick you:

"Does every internal auditor need IT expertise?" → NO, the ACTIVITY collectively must.
"Can an auditor with no IT knowledge audit IT?" → They should have GENERAL IT knowledge but can bring in specialists.
"Must internal auditors detect all fraud?" → NO, but must evaluate risk and recognize red flags.

sections.title: Due Professional Care (Standard 1220)
sections.content:
**Due professional care requires:**

• Extent of work needed to achieve objectives
• Relative complexity, materiality, and significance
• Adequacy of governance, risk, control processes
• Probability of significant errors, fraud, or noncompliance
• Cost of assurance vs. potential benefits

**Due care does NOT guarantee:**
• All errors will be found
• All fraud will be detected
• All risks will be identified

**Standard: Care and skill expected of a reasonably prudent and competent internal auditor.**

sections.title: Continuing Professional Development (1230)
sections.content:
**CPD is MANDATORY for all internal auditors:**

• Enhance knowledge, skills, and competencies
• Stay current with profession developments
• Applies regardless of experience level

**Acceptable CPD Activities:**
• Professional seminars and conferences
• Additional certifications (CIA, CISA, CFE)
• Self-study and research
• Teaching and mentoring
• Professional association activities
• On-the-job training and rotation

**CIA CPE Requirements:**
• 40 hours per year
• 2 hours must be in ethics

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Proficiency is collective — the team, not just individuals
• ALL auditors need basic IT and fraud knowledge
• Specialists fill gaps that generalists cannot
• Due professional care ≠ guarantee
• CPD is mandatory and ongoing (40 hours/year for CIA)
• Key phrase: "reasonably prudent and competent"

knowledgeCheck.question: An internal audit activity is performing an audit of a complex derivatives trading system. While none of the current internal audit staff possess specific expertise in derivatives, the CAE has contracted with a subject matter expert to assist the team. According to Standard 1210, is this an acceptable approach to achieving proficiency?
options: No, Standard 1210 requires that all internal auditors possess all necessary skills.
Yes, Standard 1210 allows the internal audit activity to obtain the necessary skills to perform its responsibilities.
No, contracting with a subject matter expert is a violation of auditor independence.
Yes, as long as the CAE documents the lack of internal expertise in the audit workpapers.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Standard 1210 emphasizes collective proficiency. The internal audit activity can either possess or obtain the necessary knowledge and skills. Contracting with an expert is a valid method of obtaining proficiency.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the difference between individual and collective proficiency.

reveal.question: Per standard 1210.A3, list three areas of IT knowledge ALL internal auditors should understand (even if they are not IT experts).
reveal.answer:
1. Key information technology risks
2. Key IT controls
3. Technology-based audit techniques

rows.label: Responsibility
values: Evaluate the risk of fraud, identify indicators, and understand fraud management.

rows.label: Expected Actions
values: Identify red flags and assess the adequacy of fraud controls.
Gather evidence, interview witnesses, and determine the extent of fraud.

title: Code of Ethics: Applied Scenarios
description: Master the four ethical principles through real-world scenarios and application

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The Code of Ethics is heavily tested on the CIA exam. Questions present ethical scenarios and ask you to identify which principle was violated or which action is appropriate. Knowing the principles AND their Rules of Conduct is critical.

sections.title: Code Structure
sections.content:
**The Code has two components:**

1. **Principles** — Broad expectations (4 principles)
2. **Rules of Conduct** — Specific behavioral requirements under each principle

**Applies to:**
• All IIA members and CIA holders
• ALL individuals providing internal audit services (even non-members)
• External providers performing internal audit work

sections.title: The Four Principles: I-O-C-C
sections.content:
**1. INTEGRITY**
• Perform work with honesty, diligence, and responsibility
• Observe the law and make disclosures expected by law/profession
• Shall not knowingly participate in illegal/discreditable activities
• Respect and contribute to legitimate objectives of organization

**2. OBJECTIVITY**
• Shall not participate in activities that may impair unbiased assessment
• Shall not accept anything that may impair professional judgment
• Disclose all material facts known that could distort reporting

**3. CONFIDENTIALITY**
• Prudent use and protection of information
• Shall not use information for personal gain or contrary to law/objectives

**4. COMPETENCY**
• Engage only in services for which they have necessary knowledge
• Continually improve proficiency and effectiveness
• Follow applicable professional standards

sections.title: Scenario Application Practice
sections.content:
**Scenario 1:** An auditor owns stock in a company being audited.
→ Violation: **Objectivity** (financial interest creates impairment)

**Scenario 2:** An auditor discusses audit findings at a dinner party.
→ Violation: **Confidentiality** (disclosing without authorization)

**Scenario 3:** An auditor agrees to audit complex derivatives without expertise.
→ Violation: **Competency** (engaging in services beyond skill level)

**Scenario 4:** An auditor finds evidence of fraud but doesn't report it.
→ Violation: **Integrity** (failing to make expected disclosures)

**Scenario 5:** An auditor accepts a gift from the department being audited.
→ Violation: **Objectivity** (accepting something that may impair judgment)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Strategy: Ethical Scenarios
sections.content:
When faced with ethics scenarios:

1. Identify which principle is at risk
2. Look for the MOST DIRECT violation
3. Remember: Integrity = honesty/courage; Objectivity = unbiased/no conflicts; Confidentiality = information protection; Competency = skills/standards
4. If two principles seem to apply, choose the one MOST DIRECTLY related

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Four principles: Integrity, Objectivity, Confidentiality, Competency (I-O-C-C)
• Each principle has specific Rules of Conduct
• Applies to ALL internal audit service providers
• Violations may lead to IIA disciplinary action
• Exam tests through scenario-based questions
• When in doubt: integrity and objectivity are most fundamental

knowledgeCheck.question: An internal auditor discovers that a senior manager is consistently exaggerating expense reports by small amounts. The auditor, fearing reprisal from the manager who is influential within the organization, decides to ignore the discrepancies and not report them. Which principle of the IIA's Code of Ethics has the auditor most clearly violated?
options: Objectivity
Confidentiality
Integrity
Competency
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The auditor's decision to ignore the discrepancies and not report them demonstrates a lack of honesty and responsibility, which directly violates the principle of Integrity. Integrity requires internal auditors to perform their work with honesty, diligence, and responsibility.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which principle focuses on honesty and ethical conduct.

reveal.question: What are the two main components of the IIA's Code of Ethics?
reveal.answer: The two main components are: 1. Principles (broad expectations) and 2. Rules of Conduct (specific behavioral requirements).

rows.label: Focus
values: Impartiality, intellectual honesty, freedom from conflicts of interest

rows.label: Potential Violation Examples
values: Knowingly participating in illegal activities; failing to report known wrongdoing
Auditing an area where the auditor has a close personal relationship; owning stock in an auditee
